shortcut: A Dutch sentence has slots, and every slot has a name. Once you can label SUBJECT, VERB, OBJECT, INFORMATION, you can read every Dutch grammar rule fluently.
explanation: Word-types tell you what each word IS (verb, noun, etc — see the Word Types glossary). Sentence-anatomy tells you what each word DOES in this particular sentence (subject, object, information, etc). The two perspectives sit on top of each other. Belgian-Dutch teachers and textbooks use the sentence-anatomy labels constantly — especially when explaining inversion, separable verbs, and subordinate clauses. Here are the labels you'll hear, with English translations and examples that highlight the parts.

examples: Who or what is DOING the action of the verb? Answers 'who?' or 'what?' before the verb.
English: 'The dog runs.' — 'the dog' is the subject.
Dutch: 'De hond loopt.' — 'de hond' is the subject (onderwerp).
🇳🇱 Two Dutch names: 'onderwerp' (under-thrown — the thing being talked about) is the everyday Dutch term. 'subject' is the Latin term used in formal grammar tables.
In Dutch the subject is USUALLY at position 1, but inversion moves it AFTER the verb.

patterns.rule: VERB / CONJUGATED VERB · de persoonsvorm · het verbum
examples: The CONJUGATED verb (changed to match the subject) — the one carrying the tense and person.
English: 'The dog RUNS.' — 'runs' is the conjugated verb (it changed from 'run' to match 'the dog').
Dutch: 'De hond LOOPT.' — 'loopt' is the persoonsvorm (changed from 'lopen' to match 'de hond').
🇳🇱 Two names: 'persoonsvorm' (person-form — the verb shaped to fit the person doing it) and 'verbum' (Latin).
⚠ The persoonsvorm is what sits at POSITION 2 in every Dutch main clause. The V2 rule is really 'the conjugated verb is the anchor at slot 2'.

patterns.rule: OBJECT · het lijdend voorwerp · het object
examples: The thing that RECEIVES the action of the verb. Answers 'what?' or 'whom?' after the verb.
English: 'The dog eats the BONE.' — 'the bone' is the direct object.
Dutch: 'De hond eet HET BOTJE.' — 'het botje' is the lijdend voorwerp.
🇳🇱 Literal translation of 'lijdend voorwerp' = 'suffering object' — the thing that the action 'happens to'.
There's also an INDIRECT OBJECT (meewerkend voorwerp) — the receiver, usually 'to whom?' or 'for whom?': 'I give the bone to the DOG.' = 'Ik geef het botje AAN DE HOND.'
EVERYTHING else in the sentence — time, place, manner, reason. Things that ADD detail but aren't subject, verb, or object.
English: 'The dog runs FAST IN THE PARK ON SATURDAYS.' — 'fast' (manner), 'in the park' (place), 'on Saturdays' (time) are all information.
Dutch: 'De hond loopt SNEL IN HET PARK OP ZATERDAGEN.' — same three types of information.
🇳🇱 Belgian-Dutch teachers casually call all of this 'informatie' (informatie). The formal term is 'bepaling' (modifier/adjunct), but 'informatie' is what you'll hear in class.
ANY piece of information can be moved to position 1 of the sentence — that's the trigger for INVERSION.
→ Full inversion rule: [[inversion]].

patterns.rule: TIME information — the most common inversion trigger
examples: TIME information answers 'when?' / 'how often?' / 'how long?'.
Single words: vandaag, morgen, gisteren, nu, soms, altijd, vaak, nooit, vanavond.
Clock phrases: om acht uur, om kwart voor zes, om half tien.
Days/dates: op maandag, op 8 september, in januari.
Periods: in het weekend, in de zomer, deze week.
Time-of-day: 's morgens, 's middags, 's avonds, 's nachts.
Frequency: elke dag, elke avond, twee keer per week.
⚠ Any of these at slot 1 → INVERSION. The most-used trigger by far.
→ Full inversion rule with worked examples: [[inversion]].
A complete sentence that can stand on its own.
English: 'I am going home.' (full sentence, makes sense alone)
Dutch: 'Ik ga naar huis.' (full sentence)
The V2 rule applies to MAIN clauses: the verb sits at position 2.
🇳🇱 Literally 'hoofdzin' = 'head sentence' (the main one).
A clause that DEPENDS on a main clause — usually introduced by a subordinating conjunction (omdat, dat, als, wanneer, terwijl, hoewel).
English: 'I am going home BECAUSE I AM TIRED.' — 'because I am tired' is the subordinate clause.
Dutch: 'Ik ga naar huis OMDAT IK MOE BEN.' — 'omdat ik moe ben' is the bijzin.
⚠ HUGE Dutch rule: in a bijzin, the conjugated verb moves to the END of the clause. 'omdat ik moe BEN' (because I tired AM), 'dat hij niet kan KOMEN' (that he not can COME).
🇳🇱 'bijzin' = 'beside sentence' (one that sits next to the main one).
→ Full word-order rules including subclauses: [[wordorder]].
Dutch teachers literally count slots in a main clause:
Position 1 = ONE element (subject, time, place, question word, or a subordinate clause).
Position 2 = the CONJUGATED VERB. Always. No exceptions in main clauses.
Position 3+ = the rest of the sentence — subject (if not at 1), objects, information, infinitives at the end.

examples.caption: Modal at position 2 → infinitive closes the clause

examples: → Inversion in full: [[inversion]].
A verb made of TWO parts: a prefix (op-, uit-, aan-, mee-, thuis-) and a base verb.
Listed in the dictionary as one word: 'opstaan' (to get up), 'uitgaan' (to go out), 'aandoen' (to put on).
In a MAIN clause, the two parts SPLIT — the base verb sits at position 2, the prefix goes to the END of the clause.
→ Full separable-verb rules: [[separable]].

patterns.rule: INFINITIVE AT THE END — the modal-verb pattern
examples: When a sentence has TWO verbs (modal + infinitive), they SPLIT across the clause:
Conjugated modal → position 2.
Infinitive → very end of the clause.
Example: 'Vandaag wil ik thuis werken.'
Pos 1: Vandaag | Pos 2: wil (modal) | Pos 3: ik | Pos 4: thuis | End: werken (infinitive)
Same with kunnen/moeten/mogen/zullen.
→ Modal-verb rules: [[modal]].

memory: 🧠 Two glossaries, two perspectives. The Word-Types glossary [[terms_words]] tells you what a word IS (verb, noun, pronoun…). The Sentence-Anatomy glossary tells you what a word DOES in a sentence (subject, object, information…). Master both labels for any single word in a sentence and you can read every Dutch grammar rule fluently. The most important sentence-anatomy concept for Dutch: COUNT TO TWO — what's at position 1, what's at position 2. The conjugated verb is ALWAYS at position 2 in main clauses.

examples.caption: Separable verb under inversion — prefix at the END
Modal sentence — infinitive at the END
Main clause + subclause — verb 'ben' kicked to the END after 'omdat'
examples.note: Main clause keeps V2 (verb at pos 2). Subclause sends its conjugated verb to the END.

practice.nl: Welk woord is het onderwerp in 'Vandaag werk ik thuis'?
practice.en: Which word is the subject in 'Vandaag werk ik thuis'?

practice.nl: 'Ik' is het onderwerp (subject). Het zit aan positie 3 omdat 'vandaag' (informatie) aan positie 1 staat — dat is inversie.
practice.en: 'Ik' is the subject. It's at position 3 because 'vandaag' (information) is at position 1 — that's inversion.

practice.nl: De persoonsvorm is de geconjugeerde vorm van het werkwoord — de vorm die past bij het onderwerp. In het Nederlands staat de persoonsvorm altijd op positie 2 in een hoofdzin.
practice.en: The persoonsvorm is the conjugated form of the verb — the form that fits the subject. In Dutch, the persoonsvorm always sits at position 2 in a main clause.

practice.nl: Wat is het verschil tussen een hoofdzin en een bijzin?
practice.en: What is the difference between a 'hoofdzin' and a 'bijzin'?

practice.nl: Hoofdzin = main clause, kan alleen staan. Bijzin = subordinate clause, hangt aan een hoofdzin. In een bijzin staat het werkwoord aan het EINDE: '...omdat ik moe BEN.'
practice.en: Hoofdzin = main clause, can stand alone. Bijzin = subordinate, attaches to a main clause. In a bijzin the verb goes to the END.

practice.nl: Wat is 'informatie' in een Nederlandse zin?
practice.en: What is 'informatie' in a Dutch sentence?

practice.nl: Informatie = everything that isn't subject, verb, or object. Tijd, plaats, manier. ALLE informatie kan op positie 1 staan en dat veroorzaakt inversie.
practice.en: Information = everything that isn't subject, verb, or object. Time, place, manner. ANY piece of information can go to position 1, and that causes inversion.

practice.nl: Identificeer de delen in: 'Op zondag wandelen wij in het park.'
practice.en: Identify the parts in: 'Op zondag wandelen wij in het park.'

practice.nl: Pos 1: 'Op zondag' (informatie — tijd). Pos 2: 'wandelen' (persoonsvorm — verbum). Pos 3: 'wij' (onderwerp). Pos 4+: 'in het park' (informatie — plaats). Klassieke inversie.
practice.en: Pos 1: 'Op zondag' (time info). Pos 2: 'wandelen' (conjugated verb). Pos 3: 'wij' (subject). Pos 4+: 'in het park' (place info). Classic inversion.
